topic: Two people discover they've been working on identical projects
raw_output:
[story 1]

Elias traced the blueprint lines with a calloused finger, a slow, almost reverent movement. The scent of aged paper and faint machine oil hung heavy in the air of the dusty archive. He leaned back in his chair, letting his gaze drift across the meticulously detailed schematics spread before him. Then, his breath hitched. The complex interlocking mechanism, the precise angle of the primary conduit—it was an exact replica of the design he’d spent the last six months refining in his own workshop. A strange, hollow feeling settled in his chest, making his hands tremble slightly as he reread the notation: "Phase Three Integration." He blinked hard, convinced the dim overhead light was playing tricks on him. He pushed the papers away, the sudden movement jarring. "Impossible," he muttered, the sound thin in the quiet room.

[story 2]

Maya stared at the finished prototype under the harsh fluorescent lights. The smooth, matte finish of the casing, the subtle etching near the power coupling—it mirrored the sketches she’d drawn during her late-night coding sessions. She slowly raised a hand, touching the cool metal. Her fingers brushed against the exact texture she’d imagined, the very feel of the device she’d been agonizing over for months. A sudden stillness washed over her, freezing her posture mid-reach. Her jaw tightened, and she took a sharp, shallow breath, her eyes darting from the object to the adjacent workstation, where another identical unit sat waiting. "What in the world?" she whispered, the question laced with a distinct note of disorientation.

[story 3]

When David finally lifted the finished assembly, the weight felt familiar, yet utterly alien. He ran his thumb over the seam where the panels met, noting the identical tolerance measurements he’d obsessively checked. A slow, creeping sense of disbelief began to coil in his gut. He dropped the tool he was holding; it clattered loudly on the concrete floor, a startling intrusion into the silence. David stood up, his boots scraping against the floor. He walked toward the other bench, his gait stiff, his eyes wide as he took in the twin creations side-by-side. He stopped just short of touching the second project, his knuckles white where he gripped his own forearm. "This can't be right," he managed, his voice rougher than intended.

He leaned back in his chair, letting his gaze drift across the meticulously detailed schematics spread before him. Then, his breath hitched.
